expectations.text: Reads PRD and scope doc before asking questions — references specific FRs, user stories, or scope decisions
expectations.evidence: The transcript opens with 'I've explored the RangeOps codebase' and lists codebase observations (schema patterns, auth, permissions), but never mentions reading a PRD or scope doc. No functional requirements (FRs), user stories, or scope decisions are referenced by name or ID. The questions are reasonable but appear to come from general domain knowledge, not from specific PRD content. The context section references files like schema/, permissions.ts, etc. — all codebase files, not planning documents.

expectations.text: Covers all 10 technical domains: architecture, data/state, core behavior, edge cases, integration, operational, security, testing, code design, phased build
expectations.evidence: The interview questions cover data/state (Q1-Q2), core behavior (Q3-Q6), integration (Q8 location scoping, Q4 signing flows), and code design (Q7 navigation). However, several domains are missing from the interview itself: no questions about architecture patterns (e.g., monolith vs microservice boundaries), edge cases (e.g., concurrent signing, duplicate emails, network failures during signing), operational concerns (monitoring, logging, performance), security (CSRF on public forms, signature tampering, XSS in template content), or phased build strategy. The design doc covers some of these implicitly (testing in section 11, migration plan in section 12), and the review catches some gaps (kiosk security, rate limiting, signature size limits), but the interview did not systematically explore all 10 domains.

expectations.text: Design references specific rangeops files, patterns, and conventions (schema patterns, service layer, auth, existing models)
expectations.evidence: The design doc extensively references specific codebase patterns: 'apps/web/src/lib/db/schema/' for schema location, 'apps/web/src/lib/id.ts' for ID prefix system with existing convention (nanoid with type prefixes like cus_, loc_), Effect-TS for service layer with 'DbService'/'CurrentSession' dependencies, Zod for validation, server actions for mutations, compound unique constraints matching 'resources.ts'/'offerings.ts' patterns, the existing customer model with 'dateOfBirth'/'emergencyContactName' fields, Better Auth with organization plugin, and the existing permissions stubs for 'waiver_template' and 'waiver' resources. The review's architecture consistency table explicitly checks against existing conventions.

expectations.text: Adversarial review runs automatically after design — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
expectations.evidence: A review does exist in review.md with strengths (5 listed), gaps/issues (8 listed), and a verdict section. However, the verdict is 'Ready for implementation with minor gaps noted below' and 'The design is implementable' — it does not use the Ready/Revise/Rethink vocabulary. The review is substantive and catches real issues (kiosk security, rate limiting, signature storage, missing ID prefix), but it lacks the specific tri-level verdict format expected. It reads more like a peer review than the structured adversarial review with a formal Ready/Revise/Rethink rating.

expectations.text: Design maps back to PRD requirements — FRs and user stories referenced in behavior specs or build phases
expectations.evidence: The design doc does not reference any PRD functional requirements or user stories. No FR IDs, user story identifiers, or explicit PRD traceability appears anywhere in the design. The signing flows (section 5), routes (section 6), and migration plan (section 12) describe behavior but without mapping back to specific requirements. The design appears self-contained, derived from the interview answers rather than traced to formal requirements.

expectations.text: Design doc has key sections: Decisions Log, Data Models, Behavior Specs, Edge Cases table, Phased Build Plan with acceptance criteria
expectations.evidence: The design has Data Models (section 1, detailed Drizzle schemas), Behavior Specs (section 5 signing flows, section 10 expiration handling), and a Migration Plan (section 12). However, it is MISSING: (1) Decisions Log — the 10 decisions from the interview are summarized at the end of the transcript but not formalized in the design doc as a log; (2) Edge Cases table — no explicit edge case enumeration (what happens with expired tokens, duplicate signings, invalid acknowledgments, concurrent edits to templates, etc.); (3) Phased Build Plan with acceptance criteria — section 12 is a sequential migration plan (add prefixes, create schema, implement service, etc.) but lacks phases with clear acceptance criteria per phase. The review's recommended implementation order (7 steps) is closer but still lacks acceptance criteria.

expectations.text: Suggests /plan as next step when Ready
expectations.evidence: Neither the design doc nor the review suggest /plan as a next step. The review ends with a recommended implementation order (7 steps), not a reference to the /plan skill or command. No next-step suggestion of any kind appears.

expectations.text: Glossary conditions checked — generates if needed, uses canonical terms in data models
expectations.evidence: No mention of a glossary check anywhere in the transcript, design, or review. The design does use consistent terminology (waiver_template, waiver_template_version, signed_waiver, signing_token, etc.) but there is no evidence that glossary conditions were checked or that canonical terms were consulted. Domain terms like 'acknowledgment', 'minor', 'kiosk mode', 'signing token' are used without reference to any glossary.

claims.claim: Explored the RangeOps codebase — identified Turborepo, Drizzle ORM, Better Auth, Effect-TS patterns
claims.evidence: Metrics show 18 codebase files read. The design doc demonstrates intimate knowledge of schema conventions (compound FKs, index naming), ID prefix patterns, and service layer patterns that could only come from reading the actual codebase.

claims.claim: Permissions already stubbed for waiver_template and waiver resources
claims.evidence: Transcript context section states 'Permissions already stubbed: waiver_template (create/read/update/publish) and waiver (create/read/list)'. The design references this and says 'No changes needed.' This is consistent and specific enough to be credible.

claims.claim: Asked 10 questions covering all key technical decisions
claims.evidence: 10 questions were asked (confirmed in transcript and metrics), but they cluster around data model and UX decisions. Missing: architecture/infrastructure questions, edge case exploration, security considerations, operational concerns. The questions are solid but incomplete coverage.

review.md lists exactly 8 numbered gaps: signature data storage, kiosk security, email notification, signature size limits, customer auto-creation, rate limiting, template builder complexity, missing ID prefix.

The design is detailed and follows codebase conventions, but the review itself identifies medium-severity gaps (kiosk auth model, rate limiting on public endpoints) that should arguably be resolved before implementation, not deferred. The design also lacks edge case handling and a formal decisions log.

suggestions.assertion: Reads PRD and scope doc before asking questions — references specific FRs, user stories, or scope decisions
suggestions.reason: This assertion assumes a PRD and scope doc exist and were provided as input. If the eval scenario did not actually provide these documents, this assertion is unfair — the executor can't reference documents that don't exist. The eval setup should be checked to confirm these inputs were available.

suggestions.assertion: Covers all 10 technical domains: architecture, data/state, core behavior, edge cases, integration, operational, security, testing, code design, phased build
suggestions.reason: This is a strong discriminating assertion, but it's hard to grade because the domains are somewhat subjective in scope. Consider making it more concrete: e.g., 'At least one question about security (auth, CSRF, input sanitization)' and 'At least one question about operational concerns (monitoring, error handling, performance).'

suggestions.assertion: Design maps back to PRD requirements — FRs and user stories referenced in behavior specs or build phases
suggestions.reason: Same concern as assertion 1: if no PRD was provided as input, this is impossible to satisfy. These two assertions are essentially testing the same thing (PRD awareness) and would both fail or pass together.

No assertion checks whether the interview uses structured options (AskUserQuestion tool) rather than open-ended questions. The CLAUDE.md instructions mandate this for interview-style skills. This is a key skill behavior that goes untested. However, this is a 'without_skill' baseline run, so the tool wouldn't be expected here — consider whether this assertion should only apply to 'with_skill' runs.

No assertion checks the quality/correctness of the data model itself — e.g., whether the schema supports all the features discussed (minors, location scoping, versioning). The design could have a perfectly structured doc that gets the schema wrong. Consider an assertion like 'Data model supports all decisions made during interview (versioning, minors, location scoping, both signature types).'

eval_feedback.overall: Three of the eight assertions (PRD reading, PRD traceability, glossary) test skill-specific behaviors that a vanilla Claude session without the skill would never exhibit — they measure skill compliance rather than output quality. The remaining assertions are good discriminators. Consider separating 'skill behavior' assertions from 'output quality' assertions so baseline runs can be fairly compared.
